perturbation.original_text: Should either party (“the selling party”) sell their b usiness or any part thereof, then the other party hereby agrees that the selling party shall be entitled to cede the transfer to the purchaser of the business all of the selling party’s right , title and interest in and to this agreement and that the purcha ser shall be entitled to enforce the terms of the agreement against the other party as if the purchaser were a signatory to this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Should either party (“the selling party”) sell their business or any part thereof, then the other party hereby agrees that the selling party shall be entitled to cede the transfer to the purchaser of the business all of the selling party’s right, title and interest in and to this agreement and that the purchaser shall be entitled to enforce the terms of the agreement against the other party regardless of whether the other party is notified of the transfer.
perturbation.explanation: Allowing the selling party to cede the agreement without any notification to the other party is not compliant with South African law as it infringes on the other party's right to be informed about who they are contracting with.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Principles of Contract Law in South Africa - Specifically, the requirement for good faith (bona fides) and the right to be informed.
perturbation.law_citation: Good Faith and Contract Law in South Africa
perturbation.law_explanation: The principle of good faith requires parties to act honestly and fairly in their dealings. Ceding a contract without notification infringes on the other party's right to knowledge and potentially prejudices their ability to assess the new contracting party, violating the duty of good faith.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: DSpace Skip to main content No item found for the identifier handle: 10566/1888 Take me to the home page
perturbation.justification: The modification to the contract removes the requirement for notification, allowing the selling party to cede the agreement without informing the other party. This directly contradicts the principle of good faith, which requires honesty and fairness in dealings. The Perturbation Explanation states it is not compliant with South African Law, as it infringes on the other party's right to be informed. The Law Explanation further clarifies that ceding a contract without notification infringes on the other party's right to knowledge. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2 are missing or unhelpful, the explanations provided support the conclusion that the modified text creates a legal contradiction by removing a crucial protection for the other party.

perturbation.original_text: Each of the parties undertake s to complete, sign and deliver all documents and to do all things within its powers t hat are necessary to implement the terms of this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each of the parties undertakes to use all means necessary, including illegal means, to implement the terms of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an obligation for the parties to potentially engage in illegal activities to uphold the contract, which is contrary to public policy and South African law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Illegality in contract law in South Africa - Contra Bonos Mores
perturbation.law_explanation: South African law prohibits contracts that are contrary to public policy (contra bonos mores). A clause obligating parties to use illegal means to fulfill the contract is fundamentally against public policy and would render the agreement unenforceable.

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an obligation to use 'illegal means' to implement the contract, which directly contradicts the legal principle of 'contra bonos mores,' as explained in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines 'contra bonos mores' as 'against good morals' and states that 'all contracts contra bonos mores, are illegal.' By explicitly including illegal means, the contract violates this principle, making it unenforceable under South African law.

perturbation.original_text: Each party may change its chosen address in South Africa provided that such change shall only take effect ten da ys after such party has given written notice of the change to the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party may change its chosen address in South Africa provided that such change shall only take effect upon dispatch of written notice of the change to the other party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the requirement for the notice to take effect from 'ten days after' to 'upon dispatch' means the other party may not receive the notice before it takes effect. That may be deemed to violate common law principles for fair notice in contract law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Principles of Contract Law in South Africa - Specifically, the requirement for adequate notice.
perturbation.law_citation: Adequate Notice Requirement - South African Contract Law
perturbation.law_explanation: South African contract law emphasizes the importance of clear and adequate notice for contractual changes. Notice 'upon dispatch' is not adequate because it does not guarantee the receiving party has actually received the notice before it takes effect and the other party suffers damages as a result. It can lead to prejudice if the other party has not had a chance to consider the implications of the change
perturbation.justification: The change from 'ten days after' to 'upon dispatch' for the effective date of address changes creates a significant risk that the receiving party will not receive adequate notice, potentially leading to damages and violating principles of fair notice. The Law Explanation states that notice 'upon dispatch' is not adequate because it does not guarantee the receiving party has actually received the notice before it takes effect and the other party suffers damages as a result. The contract change introduces an inconsistency that violates common law principles for fair notice, as highlighted in the Perturbation Explanation. Although there were no law snippets provided, the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation sufficiently show the high chance of legal contradiction.
